sop.coo_evaluation:
# SOP Audit & Build Plan – The Forge Belfast

## **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident handling** – no documented escalation, documentation, or resolution protocol (critical for duty of care & retention)
- **Injury reporting & modification protocols** – who documents, how coaches flag modifications, liability trail
- **Member win-back/reactivation** – trigger points, messaging cadence, staff responsibility (revenue-critical for churn-heavy fitness)
- **Financial reconciliation & refund processing** – who approves, timelines, dispute handling
- **Staff performance review & capability process** – how underperformance is managed (you have 17 coach SOPs but no accountability framework)
- **Health & safety incident log & near-miss reporting** – beyond facilities maintenance
- **Induction for new gym members** (beyond signup) – tour, equipment orientation, goal-setting session structure
- **Class cancellation protocol** – communication timeline, member credits/rebook, coach notification
- **Data security & GDPR compliance** – member data handling, retention, breach response
- **Supplier & vendor onboarding/offboarding** – repeat purchase, service SLAs

## **Needs updating**

- **Coach onboarding & probation SOP** – likely static; needs real attrition data + what actually predicts retention
- **Class scheduling & waitlist management** – if dynamic (popular classes, seasons), the SOP probably isn't
- **Member billing & payment failure** – payment reconciliation and dunning cadence likely ad-hoc
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)** – what's the gap? Likely missing **referral incentive fulfillment** or **partnership/corporate outreach process**
- **Facilities maintenance** – seasonal deep-cleans, equipment servicing schedules, replacement cycles probably underdocumented
-

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTIONS

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Pricing is soft: AR/M down to £120.48 (June) from £165.56 (May).** Your average revenue per member has collapsed 27% month-on-month despite adding 25 new clients. Audit your mix — are new joiners on lower tiers or are existing members downgrading? **Action: Tier new sign-ups to mid/premium bands (minimum £25/month uplift), and run a one-month retention offer (lock current members at +£5/month for 6 months) to recapture £45+ ARR.** This alone could add £4k–£6k monthly.

• **Churn at 0% in June masks underlying attrition: you added 25 net members but revenue fell 12%.** The new studio attracted volume, not value. **Action: Offer class bundles (12-class + PT session at £89/month vs. standard £49) and upsell personal training at sign-up.** June's client base (147) can support £180k+ annual revenue at £25 AR/M — you're at £212k run-rate.

• **Non-peak hours are underused.** 9–5 weekday slots typically have 30–40% spare capacity in a studio your size. **Action: launch a "off-peak" tier at £34/month (weekdays 9–17 only) and actively sell to local professionals; target 15 sign-ups by month-end for +£510 MRR.**

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people expenses spiked to £15.6k (March), £30.5k (April relocation), then normalised to £9.2k–£9.4k (May–June).** May–June baseline of ~£9.3k is sustainable post-move, but **June's £9.4k is still 103% of revenue-to-expense ratio on the non-people line — target ≤35% of gross revenue.** At £20k revenue, that's £7k max. **Action: audit utilities (heating a 2x studio), supplies, and insurance; renegotiate landlord or energy supplier. Quick win: move cleaning to fortnightly contractor (±£300/month).**

• **Staffing costs rose 26% (May £3.55k → June £3.69k) while revenue fell 12%.** Payroll-to-revenue jumped from 17.6% to 20.8%. **Action: shift one full-time instructor to 0

staff.advice:
• **Move Grace's PT load to JoJo and Eilis**: Grace delivered 0 PT sessions but had capacity; redistribute her 44 hours of PT delivery across JoJo (reduce her PT from 44 to ~30, assign Eilis ~14 more). This levels PT across both coaches and frees Grace entirely.

• **Shift Grace's Build: Lower (17 sessions) to JoJo**: JoJo is the strongest coach and has room. This removes Grace's largest class commitment and consolidates lower-body expertise with one reliable coach.

• **Hand Grace's Elevate and Strongevity to Eilis**: Eilis has capacity and these classes (9 sessions total) suit her current level. Builds her class variety and reduces Grace's delivery to zero.

• **Bring Eilis into Mobility and Circuits as secondary cover**: JoJo runs both (7 and 6 sessions) solo. Train Eilis to co-deliver or take alternate weeks, preventing burnout on JoJo and cross-skilling the team.

• **Assign remaining InBody scans fairly**: Grace's 9 scans and JoJo's 20 scans should distribute to Eilis (free up ~15 admin slots from JoJo). Scans take minimal time but help balance perceived workload.

• **Keep JoJo on Tuesdays (non-negotiable)**: She is managing the technically hardest day; confirm Eilis covers Mon/Wed/Thu/Fri strength classes, freeing JoJo for PT and specialist sessions.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time (gaps between sessions):**

1. **Block out a "Drop-in Barbell Clinic" on Monday/Thursday afternoons** — 30-min paid sessions (£8–12) teaching technique basics for members who want coaching between structured classes. Targets the quiet days and converts dead studio time into revenue while building skills for underattended strength classes like Barbelle and Strongevity.

2. **Run a Saturday morning "Open Gym + Coffee" slot (8–10am)** — free supervised access with a barista setup. Saturdays are your quietest day; this builds habit and creates a social draw that may funnel members into other classes later.

**For the struggling classes:**

3. **Move Engine and Capacity to Tuesday/Wednesday early mornings (6–6:45am) and pair with a 2-week "new member guarantee"** — new joinees attend free, existing members get a buddy-invite incentive (free smoothie if they bring a friend). These are conditioning-focused classes that appeal to your Build crowd; positioning them before or after your peak days captures momentum, and the guarantee removes trial risk.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — Concrete Steps for Grace

• **Audit existing revenue streams THIS WEEK**: List every service you currently charge for (classes, PT, nutrition plans, challenges, merchandise, guest passes). Ask your top 3 coaches what members actually ask for but you don't offer. This data becomes your menu skeleton — no guessing.

• **Price using member willingness, not cost-plus**: Run a 5-minute poll in your gym asking members "What would you pay for [1:1 nutrition coaching]?" Don't lead the answer. Set prices at the 70th percentile response, not the average — you want to filter for serious buyers, not maximize volume at The Forge's stage.

• **Create TWO menus with different language**: Coaches need clinical detail (session length, what progression looks like, cancellation policy). Members need benefit-led copy ("Confidence in the gym" not "form correction drills"). Design them separately or coaches will sound robotic to members.

• **Make one price anchor obvious and slightly premium**: Your "signature" offering (maybe 12-week transformation or monthly unlimited classes) should be 15-20% pricier than your entry point. People navigate to it when they see cheaper options — this increases perceived value of everything.

• **Publish this on your site + print for the gym floor by end of month**: Ambiguity kills sales. Every member should see the same menu at point-of-decision. Print it, laminate it, post at the desk. No exceptions.

What's your current pricing for 1:1 coaching vs. group classes?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Execution for The Forge

• **Audit your actual drop-off points first** — Don't redesign from scratch. Pull analytics on where visitors leave (homepage? pricing? class details?). Ask 5-10 recent sign-ups what confused them AND ask 5 who didn't convert what stopped them. This tells you what to fix, not what you think needs fixing.

• **Build a "Women-Only Gym" value ladder on your homepage above the fold** — Grace, specificity wins. Don't say "empowering fitness." Say: "No intimidation. No mansplaining. Changing rooms & showers designed for us. Classes led by women coaches." Then link each to proof (testimonials, coach bios, class schedule).

• **Create a simple FAQ section answering the 3-5 questions your staff ACTUALLY gets asked most** — Ring your receptionists. What do enquiries ask before booking a tour? (Childcare? Parking? Beginner-friendly? Cost breakdown?) Write answers that sound like YOU, not a corporate template. This kills objections before they form.

• **Make your CTA (call-to-action) match the funnel stage** — Your homepage CTA shouldn't be "Join Now" if someone doesn't know your hours or class types yet. Use "See Our Classes" → landing page with schedule → "Book a Free Tour" → payment. Remove friction at each step.

• **A/B test ONE thing at a time for 2 weeks** — Change your main headline or add the FAQ section. Measure form submissions. Don't redesign everything simultaneously or you won't know what moved the needle.

What's your biggest source of visitor traffic right now?

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System for The Forge

**Here's the brass tacks execution:**

• **Start stupidly simple — one question only.** "What's ONE thing we could do better this month?" on a printed card at checkout or via a 30-second Google Form link. Grace, don't overthink this. Monthly surveys with 10 questions die. People won't fill them. One question gets answered, generates insight, shows you're listening.

• **Make it a post-class ritual, not a chore.** Train your instructors to ask the question verbally during cool-down for 20 seconds — "Drop your name and one idea in the box if you've got it." People talk easier than they write. You'll get gold from casual feedback instructors hear anyway; now it's captured.

• **Assign ownership: One staff member reads/collates weekly, reports back to Grace monthly.** Without this, feedback piles up and dies. Someone owns it. They spend 20 mins Friday summarizing themes (equipment gaps, class times, music complaints, whatever). Grace gets a one-page summary, not a mess.

• **Close the loop publicly.** Next month, announce ONE thing you fixed based on feedback. "Members asked for 6am classes—we're running a trial." Sounds obvious, but most gyms collect feedback and vanish. This builds trust and shows feedback actually moves needles.

• **Measure: Track complaint trends over 3 months.** Are equipment requests repeating? Schedule pain-points? This data justifies your next investment or class expansion to Grace's accountant later.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Reverse-engineer from your best performers first**
Don't start with a blank page. Shadow your top 2-3 PTs and observe their onboarding flow for PT members—what actually happens, not what "should" happen. Document this in bullet points, then formalize. This gives you SOPs rooted in what actually drives retention, not theory. Do the same for your highest-engagement Fuel Forward client.

**2. Create one master template, then populate it**
Build a single SOP skeleton (Problem → Solution → Step-by-step action → Success metric) that works across all five services. Then fill it in service-by-service. This prevents the common trap of five inconsistent documents that confuse staff and members. Each SOP should be 1-2 pages max—if it's longer, it's too detailed.

**3. Front-load the member experience, not internal operations**
Your SOPs should answer: *What does a member actually do from day 1?* (Not: What does staff do behind the scenes.) Example: "Fuel Forward SOP: Member gets welcome email within 2 hours → books first consultation call within 48 hours → receives meal plan by day 5." This clarity drives your staff's actions naturally.

**4. Assign ownership + accountability immediately**
Designate one person per service to "own" that SOP—they write it, they test it with 3 new members this month, they report back. Grace signs off. This gets it done in weeks, not months, and catches real friction points fast.

**5. Build in a 30-day review checkpoint**
Launch these SOPs, then revisit after

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar - Execution Plan for Grace

• **Batch-create monthly content in ONE session, not daily scrambling:** Block 2-3 hours on the first Monday of each month. Film 12-16 short videos at once (member transformations, workout clips, facility tours). This kills decision fatigue and ensures consistency without eating into gym operations. Use your phone—authenticity beats production quality for women's fitness.

• **Use a dead-simple template to force consistency:** Build a repeating weekly pattern: Monday (Motivation/Member Win), Wednesday (How-To/Form Tip), Friday (Challenge/Class Promo), + 1 flex post. This removes the "what do I post?" paralysis and keeps your audience in rhythm. Schedule everything in Buffer or Later the moment you film it.

• **Repurpose = 5x content ROI:** One member transformation story becomes: Instagram post + carousel + Reel + Stories + testimonial quote. One class video becomes multiple angle clips. You're not creating new content—you're slicing it differently for each platform.

• **Assign ONE person (even part-time) or tie it to someone's KPIs:** If it's nobody's job, it becomes nobody's priority. Either you own the first month to build the system, then hand it to a staff member with a simple content brief, or tie 10% of a manager's bonus to posting consistency.

• **Start with your warm database first:** Post your best content to Stories/Members-Only group initially. Test what gets response from people who know you before chasing algorithm optimization.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Execute Videography Sessions with Jordan

**Lock it in NOW with a standing contract**
- Draft a simple 6-month agreement with Jordan specifying: shoot dates (same week every 8 weeks), deliverables (3-5 short-form clips + 1 long-form piece), turnaround time (5 business days), and cost per session. This removes rescheduling friction and makes budgeting predictable.

**Build a shot list template BEFORE each session**
- 2-3 weeks out, Grace creates a "content menu" tied to current campaigns: transformation stories, class highlights, member testimonials, facility tours. Give Jordan direction so he's not improvising, and you get usable content every time—not just pretty footage.

**Designate 4-5 member "featured faces" in advance**
- Ask these members to commit to appearing on camera during that 8-week block. Brief them on why (social proof, community visibility). This removes the awkward on-the-day asks and gives you consistent, recognizable faces for your audience.

**Batch social content production the week after shoot**
- Have someone (Grace or staff) spend 1-2 hours cutting clips, adding captions, scheduling across Instagram Reels, TikTok, and LinkedIn immediately. Post 2x weekly for 4 weeks post-shoot so you stretch ROI and maintain consistency.

**Track view/engagement data per session**
- Note which content themes drive sign-ups or DMs. Feed this back to Jordan for the next shoot—he'll refine what works, and you'll stop wasting frames on content that doesn't convert.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Automating 6-Week Trial Enquiries — The Forge

**Get specifics first (do this today):**
- Pull your last 20 trial enquiries — map exactly where handoffs happen (enquiry → response → booking → first session → check-in). Identify the bottleneck (likely: delayed replies or forgotten follow-ups).
- Check what tools you're currently using (email, WhatsApp, Typeform?) — automation works best when you consolidate, not layer on new platforms.

**Build the trigger-to-action sequence:**
- Set up a Zapier/Make workflow: enquiry form submission → auto-reply within 2 hours (templates: welcome message + gym tour link + 3 booking slots) → if no booking in 24hrs, one-touch personal follow-up from you/staff
- Pre-write 3 email templates (initial response, gentle nudge, "last chance" day-5) — save them in Gmail drafts so staff only personalizes the name

**Measure what matters:**
- Track conversion: enquiries → trial starts → converts to membership (6-week window). Your automation should *shorten response time to <4 hours* — that's the real lever for conversions in fitness.
- Set a weekly 15-min audit: did anyone fall through the cracks? Use that to refine templates.

**Don't automate the relationship:**
- The first response can be automated. The *booking confirmation call* and day-1 gym buddy introduction cannot — that's where trial → member happens.

What's your current bottleneck — is it speed of response or follow-up consistency?

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist + KPI Documentation for The Forge

**1. Role-Specific Outputs (Not Generic Lists)**
- **JoJo** (assume front-of-house/member experience): Own checklists around daily member touchpoints + conversion targets. Her KPI = new member acquisition rate + member satisfaction score. Document what SHE controls daily (greeting script, trial booking process, follow-up calls), not what you do.
- **Eilis & Sarah** (assume instructors/class delivery): Their checklists = class prep, retention metrics per time slot, member progression tracking. KPIs = attendance rates + class ratings + member progression into paid tiers.

**2. Build the Checklist Template in Their Language**
- Create a one-page template (digital or printed) with 3 sections: Morning Actions | During Shift | End of Day Review
- Include actual numbers: "Book X trial sessions," "Hit Y class attendance," "Upsell Z retention packages"
- Make it tick-able so completion is visible—psychological win and accountability tool

**3. Link Checklists to Revenue Directly**
- Each action item must connect to money. E.g., JoJo's "follow up with 3 lapsed members" = X% comeback rate = £Y revenue recovered
- Share these revenue connections when you hand over checklists—they'll execute harder when they see the impact

**4. Implement Weekly Review Cadence (15 mins, Fridays)**
- 5-minute stand-up per person: What hit, what didn't, why?
- Adjust next week's checklist based on what's realistic/what moved the needle
- Document patterns

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Running Your Team Vision Session — The Forge Edition

• **Do it in two parts, not one meeting.** First 30 mins: you present SSO + 90-day plan (them listening). Second 45 mins: breakout by role (front desk, trainers, management) so each person maps their specific lane against the 90-day milestones. This prevents the "that's nice" nodding you get in big group settings.

• **Pre-assign ownership before the meeting.** Tell each team member *before* you gather which 2-3 goals from the 90-day plan they directly own (e.g., trainer owns "increase member retention by 12%," front desk owns "reduce new-member admin time by 50%"). They'll come ready to think, not defensive.

• **Make it visual and sticky.** Print the 90-day plan as a one-page timeline. Use colored tape/stickers so each person literally places their lane on the wall. Gym owners respond to concrete, not slides. Take a photo at the end — it becomes your accountability anchor.

• **Close with one question per person (not open floor).** Ask each directly: "What's one thing you need from me or the gym to crush your piece of this?" Gives you implementation blockers immediately while they're thinking clearly, not 3 weeks in when excuses have calcified.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for Grace — 30-Day Action Plan

**Week 1: Get the Real Picture (Not What People Say)**
- Have Grace spend 3 shifts shadowing each team member with a simple tally sheet: time on reception, classes taught, cleaning, admin, personal training. Don't ask them—*watch*. People underestimate their own load.
- Cross-reference against the rota—identify systematic gaps (e.g., is one instructor always covering no-shows? Is reception always drowning 6-7pm?).

**Week 2: Find the Quick Wins**
- Identify the 2-3 over-stretched roles and spot what could be stripped out or shifted: admin tasks that don't need a certified instructor, cleaning slots that could rotate, manual scheduling that software could handle.
- Don't hire yet—reallocate ruthlessly first. Often the bottleneck is one person doing three jobs.

**Weeks 3-4: Reset & Test**
- Implement new schedules for 2 weeks and *adjust daily*. Grace should touch base with each person after day 3 and day 10 specifically—not a morale chat, but "is this realistic?"
- If someone's still drowning, it's a skills gap (train them) or a culture issue (they're people-pleasing and over-committing).

**Outcome Grace Needs**: A staffing model where no one's working 10-hour gym days unpaid, class quality stays sharp, and she's freed up to sell/grow instead of firefighting.

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

**Start with staffing (likely 40-50% of gym operating costs):**
- Map actual hours worked vs. peak demand times. Shift to part-time staff during off-peak (typically 10am-4pm weekdays). If you're currently fully staffed, this alone hits 5-8%.
- Cross-train 2-3 existing staff on multiple roles (reception + cleaning + class support) so one person covers gaps—cuts overhead without reducing service quality.

**Supplier renegotiation (quick 2-3% win):**
- Call your equipment maintenance, cleaning supplies, and utilities vendors today. Say "I'm getting competitive quotes—what's your best price to keep my business?" Most will cut 5-15% just to avoid losing you.
- Switch to LED lighting if you haven't (especially relevant for gym floor). 40% energy drop on lighting = noticeable monthly savings.

**Membership model tweak (3-4% buffer):**
- Introduce a slightly lower tier (e.g., off-peak membership, no classes, or limited hours). This doesn't cut costs directly but increases conversion from lurkers to paying members—revenue growth masks any cost-cutting friction.

**Monthly tracking (this is non-negotiable):**
- Assign one person (even 3 hours/week) to track actuals vs. budget by category. The 10% target disappears without weekly visibility—costs creep back within 2 months.

**Which would move fastest for you—staffing flexibility or supplier renegotiation?** That tells us where to focus first.

# Monthly Finance KPI Update for The Forge

**What this actually requires:**
You need to pull current numbers, compare them to targets, identify what moved, and decide if you're changing course.

Here's the execution:

• **Pull 3 core metrics TODAY** — Active member count (churn %), Monthly Recurring Revenue, and Class attendance rate. Don't do a full audit; these tell you if the business is healthy. Spend 30 mins max pulling them from your booking system and bank.

• **Create a ONE-PAGE tracker** — columns for Last Month / Target / This Month / Variance. Pin it somewhere you see it daily (your office, your phone background). This becomes your real-time dashboard, not a filing exercise.

• **Set a hard trigger rule** — IF member churn hits 5%+ OR MRR drops 8%+, you pause non-urgent spending and audit what's broken that week. No waiting for month-end reviews. Action happens when numbers trigger it.

• **Block 15 mins every Friday morning** — review that week's numbers against the monthly forecast, plot where you'll land. Beats scrambling on the 30th and being reactive.

• **Link ONE metric to your staff** — Tell your class instructors their bonus/recognition moves with attendance rate. Makes KPIs real for the team, not just admin.

**The point:** This isn't bureaucracy. It's your early warning system. Do it lean and make it actionable.

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

**Get real numbers into a spreadsheet TODAY:**
- Pull last 12 months of bank statements + membership data. Don't wait for "clean" data. Messy real data beats perfect data that takes 6 weeks. Plug actuals into a simple 24-month forecast (12 historical + 12 projected).

**Build three scenarios, not one:**
- Conservative (assume 5% monthly churn, current acquisition rate): shows your worst case
- Base case (realistic: 2-3% churn, modest marketing lift): your working plan
- Growth case (what happens if you hit your referral target or run a strong campaign): your opportunity
- This takes 2 hours max. Grace uses whichever scenario fits her decision-making mood that week.

**Obsess over unit economics first:**
- Average member LTV (lifetime value): take current avg membership length × monthly fee. You probably don't know this—calculate it now.
- CAC (cost to acquire): total marketing spend last quarter ÷ new members acquired. If LTV < 3x CAC, your growth math is broken.
- These two numbers drive *everything* else. If CAC is the problem, forecasting more members is fantasy.

**Link forecasting to ONE critical lever each month:**
- Month 1: Does the model say you need 15 new members to hit targets? Make that your KPI.
- Month 2: Does it say retention is killing you? Shift focus there.
- Don't build the forecast and shelve it. Use it to decide where Grace's time goes.

**Share version 1 with your accountant in week 1:**

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Build Your Per-Service P&L (The Forge Edition)

**1. Start with 3 columns, not a spreadsheet nightmare**
— Revenue (actual cash from each service last month), Direct Costs (instructor pay, music licenses, equipment wear), Contribution Margin (the gap). Do this for: Group classes, 1-on-1 PT, retail/supplements. You'll instantly see which bleeds money and which prints it.

**2. Trap the hidden PT overhead you're missing**
— PT looks profitable until you account for studio time, consultant insurance, and admin time scheduling/messaging clients. Calculate PT profitability as: (average client rate × sessions/month) minus (instructor cost + 15% admin burden). This reveals if you're actually making £5/hour or £50/hour per trainer.

**3. Add-ons are margin gold — quantify it NOW**
— Track what % of members buy protein, bands, merch. Even if it's 10% of members at £15/item, that's pure margin (60%+ typically). If it's currently unmeasured, you're leaving £200-500/month on the table.

**4. Use this to make ONE decision this week**
— Don't map for mapping's sake. Identify your lowest-margin service and either: cut it, raise the price 15%, or bundle it with a high-margin winner. Grace, this P&L becomes actionable only if it changes behavior immediately.

**5. Repeat this monthly in 15 minutes**
— Build a simple template once, plug in actuals on the first Friday of each month. Trend it. Your profitability story changes faster than you think once women find you.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations - Concrete Action Plan

• **Run a live test TODAY**: Pick 3 fake email addresses, submit them through your website/Facebook/Instagram contact forms simultaneously. Track where each one goes, what emails they receive, timing of responses. You'll spot duplicates (same person getting 2+ welcome sequences) and breakage instantly.

• **Map the actual current state**: Export your automation list from whatever you use (Zapier/Make/native platform). For *each* automation, write down: trigger → what it does → where data lands. You'll visually see overlaps (e.g., "new enquiry" might trigger 2 different welcome sequences, or a step is broken).

• **Kill obvious duplicates ruthlessly**: If automation #3 and #7 both send "welcome to trial" emails to new enquiries, delete one. Choose the version with the best copy or timing. Don't keep both "just in case"—that's decision-avoidance.

• **Test the critical path once more**: New enquiry → welcome email → trial booking link/calendar → trial confirmation → follow-up 24hrs after trial. Personally receive these emails and click every link. If any step fails, fix it *that day* before moving on.

• **Document what works** (5-min template): Automation name, trigger, sequence, expected outcome. Share with any staff member who touches enquiries so they know what's running behind the scenes.

**Timeline: Complete this in 2-3 days max or it won't happen.**

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge: Concrete Action Plan

**1. Identify your 3 tasks FIRST (do this today)**
- **Member onboarding emails** – currently manual, repetitive, low-value time sink. Build a workflow that triggers welcome → week-1 check-in → class booking prompt automatically when new members sign up
- **Class cancellation/reschedule notifications** – likely manual texting/emailing now. Set up conditional logic: cancellation detected → auto-notify affected members with alternatives + credit offer
- **Membership renewal reminders** – if you're manually chasing lapsed members, automate: 30-days-out reminder → 7-days-out urgency → post-lapse win-back sequence

**2. Pick the EASIEST win first, not the most impactful**
- Start with onboarding emails using Zapier + your booking system. Takes 2 hours to set up, saves 5+ hours/month immediately. This builds confidence for harder automations
- Avoid anything requiring custom coding—use no-code platforms (Zapier, Make, n8n) that connect your existing tools

**3. Measure the actual time freed up**
- Track the exact minutes saved per execution across 30 days
- Reallocate that time to high-ROI work: member retention calls, partnership outreach with local businesses, community events—not just shuffling tasks around

**4. Automate *with* a human touchpoint**
- Don't go full-bot. Flag edge cases (cancellations from high-value members, non-responsive renewals) to pop into your inbox for personal follow-up
- Women's fitness is relationship-driven

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website — Execution Plan

• **Immediate audit (this week):** Run your site through free tools (SSL Labs for HTTPS check, Sucuri for malware scan). You're likely missing basics—most small gyms are. This takes 30 mins and tells you exactly what's broken before spending a penny.

• **Quick wins first:** Force HTTPS, update WordPress (if that's your CMS), disable old admin accounts, change default passwords. These plug 90% of vulnerabilities and cost nothing. Get your web host to handle it if you're not technical—they should do it free.

• **Payment security (revenue protection):** If you're taking memberships/payments on-site, verify you're PCI-compliant or use Stripe/Square's hosted checkout. A breach here tanks trust and costs thousands. This is non-negotiable.

• **Monthly maintenance contract, not one-off fix:** Budget £50-150/month for managed security (automatic backups, firewall monitoring, plugin updates). One breach costs 10x more than prevention. Delegate this—don't own it yourself.

• **Communicate the upgrade to members:** One email: "We've upgraded security to protect your payment info and data." Builds trust, differentiates you from competitors who ignore this.

**Why this matters for Grace:** Gym data breaches destroy member trust. You're losing growth potential if members see an insecure site.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your cost per lead has exploded 4.5x from April (£6) to July (£27), whilst lead volume has collapsed 94%. This isn't a gradual degradation—it's a cliff edge, suggesting creative fatigue, audience saturation, or a fundamental shift in how Meta's algorithm is treating your ads. Your close rate has actually *improved* to 75% (up from 29% in April), so the leads you're getting are higher intent, but you're buying so few that profit is down 87% month-on-month. The spend compression to £109 in July suggests you've already throttled back, which is the right instinct but incomplete—you need to diagnose *why* before you reinvest.

**Actions**

1. **Pause everything and audit creative decay.** Pull your top 3 ad creatives from April (when £6/lead worked). Check: are they still running in July? If yes, they're burnt out—audience has seen them 10+ times. If no, you've shifted creative without testing. Rebuild with fresh video or image assets (authentic gym user testimonials, before/afters from actual members, or day-in-the-life content). Test 3 entirely new creative angles: "women-only safe space" messaging, "no judgment / beginner-friendly," and "6-week transformation challenge."

2. **Expand and refresh your audience.** Your core Belfast women audience has likely been exhausted. Layer in: lookalikes built from your 20 sales in April (highest-quality cohort), interest-stack combinations beyond just "fitness" (add parenting, mental health, confidence-building), and geographic expansion to nearby towns (Lisburn, Bangor, Antrim). Test a broad interest audience to identify new user clusters Meta's algorithm finds responsive.

3. **Test offer/messaging positioning immediately.** At £27/lead, you're competing for attention but not standing out on value. Run a split test next week: keep your current "6-week trial" message on one set, but test "first week free" or "no commitment, £1 induction" on another. Measure which drives cost down. If close rate stays 70%+, the cheaper lead source wins.
